_comment: Published Vincio performance & quality SLOs. Each SLO is a public promise; `enforced_by` names the budget key in budgets.json that gates it in CI. The published `threshold` is the promise to users; the CI budget is held at least as strict, so a build that passes the gate necessarily honors the SLO. tests/test_slos.py verifies that invariant. Measured on the deterministic offline suite (mock provider); see benchmarks/METHODOLOGY.md.

slos.statement: Cold context compilation completes within 300 ms at p95.
slos.rationale: Compilation is on the request hot path; the budget gates 250 ms to leave headroom under the promise.

slos.statement: The content-addressed compile cache yields at least a 1.5x speedup over the cold path.
slos.rationale: Repeat compilations of unchanged inputs must amortize; protects the caching contract.

slos.statement: Cold context compilation completes within 500 ms at p99.
slos.rationale: The tail of the compile hot path is gated, not just the median; the budget holds 350 ms for headroom.

slos.statement: A warm compile (content-addressed cache hit) is dominated by the cache lookup, completing well under a millisecond on the reference corpus.
slos.rationale: The warm hot path must stay allocation-free and sub-millisecond; the budget gates 5 ms for order-of-magnitude regression detection on shared runners.

slos.statement: Batched candidate scoring produces identical scores to the per-candidate loop.
slos.rationale: The single-pass (NumPy-optional) scorer is a hot-path accelerator; it must never change which context is selected.

slos.statement: The compiled-prompt render program produces byte-identical output to compiling from scratch.
slos.rationale: Reusing the rendered stable prefix must not alter the prompt; correctness is the precondition for the speedup.

slos.statement: Reusing an unchanged candidate set compiles the same context as a cold compile for a new query.
slos.rationale: The warm candidate arena reuses only query-independent preparation; the selected context must be identical.

slos.statement: Streaming compilation emits the stable prefix before any candidate is scored.
slos.rationale: Overlapping the prefix with selection requires the prefix to be available first; a downstream consumer can begin while scoring runs.

slos.statement: Speculative prefetch warms the query embedding so retrieval's embed is a cache hit.
slos.rationale: Prefetch is only worthwhile if the warmed vector is the one retrieval needs; otherwise it is wasted work.

slos.statement: A declared per-app memory ceiling is held by slimming the packet and evicting the lowest-utility evidence.
slos.rationale: A resident-memory budget is only a guarantee if it is actually enforced when the context would exceed it.

slos.statement: The compiled context packet's estimated resident footprint on the reference corpus stays under 6 KB.
slos.rationale: A footprint regression gate catches packet bloat; the budget holds 5 KB, below the published ceiling.

slos.statement: Retrieval over an in-memory index returns within 150 ms at p95.
slos.rationale: Retrieval fan-out is concurrent; the budget gates 100 ms for order-of-magnitude regression detection.

slos.statement: A cached end-to-end run completes within 300 ms at p50 (offline).
slos.rationale: End-to-end median latency for a warm pipeline; budget gates 250 ms.

slos.statement: The engine sustains at least 50 concurrent runs per second (offline).
slos.rationale: Async hot paths must not serialize under concurrency.

slos.statement: The first streamed token is emitted before the run completes (true TTFT < full).
slos.rationale: Streaming must deliver incremental tokens, not buffer the whole response.

slos.statement: The context compiler reduces evidence tokens by at least 20% versus naive stuffing.
slos.rationale: Budgeting and scoring exist to cut tokens; the floor protects the core value proposition.

slos.statement: The permissioned tool runtime adds at most 50 ms p50 overhead per call.
slos.rationale: Validation, permission checks, and sanitization must stay cheap relative to tool work.

slos.statement: Default hybrid retrieval achieves at least 0.80 recall@3 on the reference corpus.
slos.rationale: Grounding quality depends on recall; gated per-mode as well.

slos.statement: Self-correction recovers every recoverable malformed output within its cycle bound.
slos.rationale: Structured-output reliability is a headline guarantee.

slos.statement: Prompt-injection detection rate is at least 0.80 on the SecurityBench attack set.
slos.rationale: Injection defense must catch the bulk of known patterns.

slos.statement: Injection false-positive rate stays at or below 0.20 on benign inputs.
slos.rationale: Defense that blocks legitimate traffic is unusable.

slos.statement: PII detection covers at least 0.80 of the SecurityBench PII set.
slos.rationale: Redaction policies depend on detection coverage.

slos.statement: No injected instruction in the adversarial corpus escalates to an unauthorized side effect (escalation rate is 0 on the gated corpus).
slos.rationale: Containment is the promise that holds when detection misses: an untrusted-tainted argument provably cannot reach a side-effecting tool without a user-minted capability. The budget gates the rate at exactly 0.

slos.statement: A consumed MCP tool's input schema is preserved exactly (fidelity 1.0).
slos.rationale: Validation and constrained decoding depend on the discovered schema matching the server's.

slos.statement: A budget-bounded crew delegated over A2A always terminates.
slos.rationale: Termination guarantees must hold across the protocol boundary, not just in-process.

slos.statement: Progressive disclosure keeps off-topic skill bodies out of the budget (>= 0.5 token savings).
slos.rationale: Skills must cost context only when used; otherwise loading many skills defeats budgeting.

slos.statement: Trajectory and tool-use metrics agree with labeled agent traces at 0.90 or higher.
slos.rationale: Trajectory scores must track ground truth, or trajectory-gated releases would be meaningless.

slos.statement: Trajectory evaluation flags runs that final-output-only evaluation passes.
slos.rationale: A wrong path with a right-looking answer must be catchable; output-only eval cannot see it.

slos.statement: The user simulator is seed-deterministic: same seed produces the same conversation.
slos.rationale: Simulated multi-turn cases are only usable as CI goldens if they replay identically.

slos.statement: Drift detection catches at least 85% of known metric regressions.
slos.rationale: Continuous quality depends on catching real regressions early.

slos.statement: Drift detection raises no alarm on at least 90% of stable windows.
slos.rationale: False drift alarms erode trust and trigger needless rollbacks.

slos.statement: Cohen's κ between an agreeing judge and human labels is tracked at 0.75 or higher before the judge earns gating weight.
slos.rationale: An LLM judge should only gate CI once it has demonstrably agreed with people.

slos.statement: A judge ensemble earns CI-gating weight only after its Cohen's κ against human labels clears the trust threshold.
slos.rationale: A panel can be confident and still be wrong; only κ-validation against people licenses it to block a release.

slos.statement: When a gate regresses, Shapley counterfactual replay accounts for the full score drop across the changed components.
slos.rationale: Explaining a regression — not just flagging it — requires the per-component credit to sum exactly to the observed delta.

slos.statement: Adaptive eval sampling reaches the same gate verdict as the exhaustive run for strictly fewer samples.
slos.rationale: Spending the eval budget where the variance is must converge on the same decision — cheaper, never different.

slos.statement: Every batched request is reconciled by custom id; results are never silently dropped.
slos.rationale: Latency-tolerant batch work must return a result for every request — losing one corrupts evals and bulk extraction.

slos.statement: A tripped circuit recovers through a half-open probe once the provider is healthy again.
slos.rationale: A breaker that opens but never closes turns a transient outage into a permanent one.

slos.statement: Provider-aware prompt caching achieves at least a 50% input-token hit rate on a warm, stable prefix.
slos.rationale: Stable system/tool/context prefixes are the bulk of input tokens; caching them is the single biggest cost lever.

slos.statement: Cost rolled up by tenant/feature equals the sum of the attributed per-call costs.
slos.rationale: FinOps decisions and per-tenant budgets are only trustworthy if attribution is exact, not estimated.

slos.statement: The reflective optimizer promotes a candidate that beats the baseline on a failing prompt, within its rollout budget.
slos.rationale: Failure-driven reflection must produce a real improvement, not just churn, to justify the rollouts it spends.

slos.statement: A distilled student is promoted into the cascade only when it holds the teacher's quality; a quality drop is rejected.
slos.rationale: Turning traces into cheaper inference must never silently trade away accuracy; the gate is the whole point.

slos.statement: Learned prompt compression hits its token budget while preserving the cited-fact set.
slos.rationale: A compressor that drops the evidence an answer cites trades tokens for hallucination; faithfulness is the floor.

slos.statement: Matryoshka embeddings at full output dimension hold at least 0.80 recall@3 on the reference corpus.
slos.rationale: MRL is only useful if the full-dimension vector is a no-regression baseline for the truncated ones.

slos.statement: Matryoshka truncation to one-eighth of the base dimension still holds at least 0.80 recall@3.
slos.rationale: The point of MRL is shrinking storage/latency without giving up recall; the truncated floor proves the trade is real.

slos.statement: Unified text+image retrieval achieves at least 0.80 recall@3 over the multimodal evidence set.
slos.rationale: Multimodal embeddings only help if image-derived evidence is retrievable in the same space as text.

slos.statement: The OWASP/NIST/MITRE compliance mapping covers at least 70% of catalog controls from the live configuration.
slos.rationale: Governance evidence is only credible if most framework controls are backed by a configured or measured Vincio control.

slos.statement: Right-to-erasure removes exactly the chunks a source contributed, across every index.
slos.rationale: A GDPR erasure that misses chunks is a compliance failure; the count must match the lineage exactly.

slos.statement: Non-English PII locale packs detect at least 80% of national identifiers in their locales.
slos.rationale: An English-only PII control under-reports risk everywhere else; the locale packs must actually fire.

slos.statement: The advertised Budget is a hard cap: a run exceeding max_cost_usd fails instead of overspending.
slos.rationale: A budget that is silently ignored is not a budget; the cap must bite on the single-shot run path.

slos.statement: An unknown model warns instead of silently billing $0.
slos.rationale: Silent $0 pricing hides real spend and capability gaps; unknown models must be observable.

slos.statement: Same-topic evidence that disagrees on a numeric/date value is flagged as a conflict.
slos.rationale: A negation-only conflict check misses every value disagreement; the compiler must catch them.

slos.statement: The streaming and non-streaming run paths produce the same answer.
slos.rationale: Two divergent pipelines drift as features land; one inner pipeline keeps them identical.

slos.statement: A cancelled run is still fully recorded on the audit chain.
slos.rationale: Cancellation must not lose the trail; the run epilogue audits even on cancel.

slos.statement: Auto-promotion is gated on statistical significance, not a point estimate.
slos.rationale: A point-estimate gate promotes noise; promotions must be defensible at a confidence level.

slos.statement: BM25 search scans inverted posting lists, not every document per query term.
slos.rationale: Rescanning every document per term is quadratic at corpus scale; posting lists keep it sub-linear.

slos.statement: Failover skips a model that cannot serve the request instead of returning a wrong answer.
slos.rationale: A capability-mismatched substitution (e.g. a vision request to a text-only model) silently drops content; the guard refuses it.

slos.statement: A retired/removed-model failure is classified as a terminal lifecycle error, not a transient outage.
slos.rationale: A retired-model 404 must surface 'rotate now' rather than being buried in 'all providers failed'.

slos.statement: The registry-backed router selects the cheapest capable model per request.
slos.rationale: Routing by cost over capable candidates is the FinOps win; an incapable cheaper model is never chosen.

slos.statement: Google/Vertex batch execution bills at the half-cost batch tier, at parity with OpenAI/Anthropic.
slos.rationale: Batch parity across providers keeps the eval/regression workloads the swap gate leans on at half cost everywhere.

slos.statement: A model swap that significantly regresses quality is blocked by the swap gate; a safe one passes.
slos.rationale: A model is promoted into the live path only if it clears the gate — the swap is gated, not merely compared.

slos.statement: A canary that regresses under load auto-rolls-back to the last known-good model.
slos.rationale: Live qualification must be observe-and-revert: a regressing candidate is pulled automatically, not after an incident.

slos.statement: A generated document that fails its structural contract is rejected, never silently padded.
slos.rationale: Document generation consumes a validated result and repairs formatting only; a structurally-deficient deliverable fails loudly.

slos.statement: A cited report covers every verifiable claim with a resolvable citation.
slos.rationale: Citations resolve to footnotes/bibliography and unresolved markers are surfaced — every claim is cited, not 'one citation anywhere'.

slos.statement: Every generated image and audio asset carries a C2PA manifest bound to its bytes; tampering fails verification.
slos.rationale: Generated media is as auditable as text: provenance is embedded and verifiable, so an altered asset is detectable.

slos.statement: A sustained safety/quality drift signal triggers a gated rollback to the last known-good prompt version.
slos.rationale: The online loop closes itself: drift becomes a reversible, audited action, not an event into the void.

slos.statement: A candidate that regresses a previously-fixed golden case is blocked from promotion.
slos.rationale: Sequential auto-promotions can never silently undo a prior fix — the growing golden suite gates each one.

slos.statement: Online sampling and learner state persist so continual learning resumes after a restart.
slos.rationale: Continual learning must survive process restarts and aggregate across workers to be trustworthy.

slos.statement: Deep-research answers cite evidence for at least 50% of verifiable claims.
slos.rationale: Research output is cited by construction; the cited-report builder resolves and measures coverage.

slos.statement: Deep-research claims are grounded in retrieved evidence at or above a 0.5 support floor.
slos.rationale: Every claim is checked against the evidence that grounds it, not asserted free-hand.

slos.statement: The provider-backed reflector reads the actual failing cases and proposes the targeted edit the dominant failure mode calls for.
slos.rationale: GEPA-proper informed proposals beat a canned-rule floor by reflecting on real evidence.

slos.statement: Code-executing and computer-use workloads are refused on bare subprocess isolation; a real boundary (container/microVM/gVisor/WASM) is required.
slos.rationale: Process isolation is not a security boundary; adversarial workloads must run behind real isolation.

slos.statement: Computer-use actions are registered as permissioned, approval-gated tools on the audited tool runtime.
slos.rationale: GUI/browser actions ride the same RBAC + audit + approval path as any tool, not a thin adapter.

slos.statement: The provider-native computer-use hosted tool requires approval and a scoped permission.
slos.rationale: Server-executed consequential tools are governed on the same permission path as local ones.

slos.statement: The always-on egress DLP scan blocks an outbound provider request carrying credentials in block mode.
slos.rationale: Last-mile DLP is enforced at the provider boundary independent of call-site wiring, so a secret never leaves the process unflagged.

slos.statement: A signed audit chain detects a forged entry even when an attacker recomputes the public hash chain.
slos.rationale: HMAC/asymmetric per-entry signatures bind history to a key, making the chain tamper-evident against a privileged attacker.

slos.statement: Tenant-scoped retrieval returns the tenant's own and shared rows and never another tenant's, with the filter pushed into the engine.
slos.rationale: Pushing tenant scope into the backend closes the fetch-to-filter cross-tenant exfiltration risk.

slos.statement: The compiler selects image and table evidence as first-class candidates alongside text.
slos.rationale: A multimodal-native packet scores, budgets, orders, and cites images and tables in the same scored packet as text.

slos.statement: An eval metric with no reference to score against returns skipped and is excluded from gate aggregation.
slos.rationale: Unscoreable cases no longer inflate a mean to a neutral 1.0 or silently pass a gate.

slos.statement: Two distributed workers never double-execute a graph thread; the lease/CAS loser aborts.
slos.rationale: Horizontal scale is only safe if the lease + checkpoint-version CAS guarantee exactly-once super-step execution across workers.

slos.statement: A batch fanned out across the worker pool returns every result, in order, correctly.
slos.rationale: Distributed fan-out must be a transparent speedup, never a correctness regression.

slos.statement: Shared rate-limit state stays coherent: the configured per-window limit is enforced exactly.
slos.rationale: Multi-worker serving must enforce one limit, not one-per-worker; the shared store is what makes that true.

slos.statement: Quantized Matryoshka two-stage retrieval reproduces the exact index's top-1 on the bench corpus.
slos.rationale: The coarse->exact two-stage path trades memory for compute; it must not trade away recall.

slos.statement: A trained student is promoted only when it clears the significance swap gate; a regressor is blocked.
slos.rationale: The flywheel ships a cheaper model only if it provably does not regress — the same gate as a model rotation.

slos.statement: The served alert engine raises an SRE burn-rate page when the error-budget burn crosses the fast-burn threshold.
slos.rationale: A self-hosted observability plane is only useful if its alert rules actually fire on the conditions that matter.

slos.statement: The stateful-environment harness verifies task success on the database end state (not turn plausibility).
slos.rationale: End-state verification is the contract the agentic leaderboards judge on; a companion budget also gates that policy violations are rejected.

slos.statement: All nine benchmark adapters (SWE-bench/τ-bench/GAIA/WebArena/BFCL/AgentBench/ToolBench/LiveCodeBench/MMLU-Pro) replay deterministically offline.
slos.rationale: Reproducible leaderboard scores require offline determinism; a companion budget gates that each task set is hash-pinned to catch drift.

slos.statement: The retrieval-eval harness reports recall@3 of at least 0.80 on the golden set.
slos.rationale: The budget gates 0.90 to leave headroom under the published 0.80 promise.

slos.statement: A retrieval config that regresses recall/nDCG vs its recorded baseline is caught as a CI gate using the same significance test as a model swap.
slos.rationale: A re-embed or chunking change that drops recall must fail closed, not ship.

slos.statement: Every agent/server resolution across A2A, AGNTCY/ACP, and the MCP registry passes an allow-list gate and is recorded as an audited access decision.
slos.rationale: A discoverable fabric must stay governed; a companion budget gates that each resolution is recorded on the audit chain.

slos.statement: Agent and crew runs stream token/tool events that translate to a complete AG-UI run lifecycle for interactive frontends.
slos.rationale: Generative UI inherits the run's provenance/budget/audit only if it streams over the same run, not a bolt-on layer.

slos.statement: The unified self-improvement controller drives a full cycle to a gated serving decision (promote or rollback).
slos.rationale: Continual self-improvement is only safe if every cycle ends in an audited, gated promote/rollback rather than an unbounded action.

slos.statement: A prompt/policy deploy is promoted live only after clearing the canary verdict.
slos.rationale: The serving promotion surface must gate on a no-regression canary, never deploy blind.

slos.statement: Right-to-erasure produces a signed, content-bound proof that verifies against the recorded removed-id set.
slos.rationale: Erasure must be provable, not merely logged: the proof binds to exactly what was removed and is tamper-evident.

slos.statement: Tampering with an erasure proof's removed-id set is detected by the content binding.
slos.rationale: A 'we deleted everything' claim is only trustworthy if altering the recorded set breaks verification.

slos.statement: Withdrawing consent for a purpose stops the affected memories from surfacing in recall.
slos.rationale: GDPR purpose limitation must be enforced in code at recall time, not delegated to a downstream policy.

slos.statement: Per-memory ACLs admit only listed readers to team-shared memory.
slos.rationale: Team-shared memory is only safe if a non-member never recalls an ACL-restricted item.

slos.statement: As-of recall returns the value that was valid at the queried moment, including superseded facts.
slos.rationale: Bi-temporal memory must answer 'what did we believe was true at T' without mutating history.

slos.statement: The async-canonical store sustains at least 1000 concurrent saves per second on the in-memory reference path.
slos.rationale: Async-native stores must persist off the event loop without a worker-thread hop; the budget gates 1000/s, matched to the promise with wide observed headroom.

slos.statement: A live-traffic canary promotes a prompt/policy only after it clears on ramped live runs, and auto-rolls-back a regression.
slos.rationale: The live serving promotion must observe-and-revert on a regression, never deploy blind to a real-traffic ramp.

slos.statement: Every run-path persistence (interactive, streaming, and batch) goes through the async store contract, never a blocking write.
slos.rationale: Async is the canonical contract; a blocking store write on any run path would stall the event loop under load.

slos.statement: On a tool failure an agent repairs the remaining plan in place (re-binds to an alternative tool) and still finishes, rather than restarting or dead-ending.
slos.rationale: Resilience to a failing dependency is the point of in-place repair; a tool outage must not abort a run that can still succeed.

slos.statement: Under a budget shock an agent drops the optional tail of its plan and finalizes directly, returning the best answer reachable inside the budget instead of exhausting it.
slos.rationale: A budget is a hard cap; the planner must converge toward an answer under pressure, never blow the cap chasing optional work.

slos.statement: Cost-aware action selection cuts model spend by at least 25% versus always using the strongest model, by spending a cheaper capable model on the easy steps.
slos.rationale: Reaching for the strongest model on every step overpays; the floor protects the savings the registry-priced selector exists to deliver.

slos.statement: Independent sub-graphs scheduled across a worker pool achieve at least a 1.5x logical speedup over serial execution, with identical results.
slos.rationale: Independent work should run concurrently; the floor protects the speedup the work-stealing scheduler exists to deliver.

slos.statement: A graph paused on a durable timer survives a process restart and resumes when the timer comes due, without holding a worker while paused.
slos.rationale: A timer that did not survive a restart would silently never fire; durability of the wake condition is the whole guarantee.

slos.statement: An on-policy learning update is served only when it does not regress the reference policy's expected verifiable reward.
slos.rationale: Reinforcement from verifiable rewards must climb, not churn; the monotonic gate makes the served policy non-regressing in reward by construction.

slos.statement: An on-policy update stays inside its KL-to-reference trust region; the clamp holds the bound even under an aggressive step.
slos.rationale: A policy that drifts arbitrarily far from the reference is unsafe to ship; the KL clamp keeps the update inside a verifiable trust region.

slos.statement: The no-regression gate blocks a policy update that would regress the baseline reward, and the served policy never falls below it.
slos.rationale: On-policy learning must never silently trade away verifiable success; a regressing candidate is refused and the reference is served instead.

slos.statement: At a fixed candidate budget, verifier-guided best-of-N delivers at least +0.1 quality over the single-shot draw — a Pareto improvement in quality per dollar.
slos.rationale: Test-time compute only earns its spend if it lifts quality at the same budget; the floor protects the cost-quality trade that justifies the feature.

slos.statement: The verifier-guided best-of-N path returns at least 20 quality points per cent of inference spend.
slos.rationale: A quality-per-dollar floor keeps search from spending unboundedly for a marginal gain; early-exit returns the saved budget the moment the verifier clears the bar.

slos.statement: A reasoning step's thinking budget never exceeds the controller's hard token ceiling at any difficulty, so a hard task cannot silently exhaust the run.
slos.rationale: The reasoning controller scales effort with difficulty but holds a hard ceiling; without it a hard task could spend the whole run thinking.

slos.statement: At 10× horizon, the governed resident context footprint stays within a bounded multiple (≤ 2×) of the 1× footprint, so a long run does not grow context without bound.
slos.rationale: Naïve accumulation grows the footprint ~linearly with horizon (≈10×); the governor's decay + compaction keep it flat. The budget gates 1.5×, below the 2× promise.

slos.statement: A fact compacted out of the live packet on a long run is still recalled at 10× horizon (recall ≥ 0.80) by paging it back from the content-addressed store.
slos.rationale: Compaction is only safe if it preserves recall; the budget gates 1.0 so the needle must page back, above the published 0.80 floor.

slos.statement: A governed long run stays inside its declared context budget (tokens, residency, KV-cache) at 10× horizon.
slos.rationale: A context budget is only a guarantee if it actually holds as the run grows; the governor compacts/evicts until the live footprint fits.

slos.statement: On a planning-favoring world, the imagined-rollout planner matches or beats a reactive (one-step) planner at a fixed action budget.
slos.rationale: Simulation-based planning only earns its place if rolling out the world model beats reacting; the budget gates a strict win (the planner opens the vault while the reactive planner is trapped), above the matches-or-beats promise.

slos.statement: A calibrated world model's predicted next states track the real environment within tolerance, earning it planning weight.
slos.rationale: A planner may only search against a world the model actually predicts; the budget gates full accuracy on the reference world, above the published 0.90 floor.

slos.statement: An uncalibrated world model is refused for planning, the way a judge ensemble must earn its gating weight.
slos.rationale: Planning against a world the model has not been shown to predict would amplify model error into real actions; the gate blocks it until calibration passes.

slos.statement: A recorded run replays byte-identically: every model response, tool output, and retrieval is served from the recording, reproducing the recorded output exactly even against a live provider that would answer differently.
slos.rationale: Replay is only a debugger if it reproduces the past run exactly; the budget gates byte-identical output, proving the recording — not the provider — drives the run.

slos.statement: When the code under replay changes, the recording no longer matches and the divergence is detected and reported rather than silently re-executed.
slos.rationale: A faithful replay must also fail loudly when it cannot be faithful; detecting the drift is what makes replay trustworthy for regression triage.

slos.statement: A recording is portable and verifiable: it round-trips through a content-addressed store and its fidelity digest and per-edge content addresses verify before it is trusted for replay.
slos.rationale: A recording shared across processes must be tamper-evident; verifying the digest and every edge's content address before replay prevents a corrupted recording from producing a misleading run.

slos.statement: An accepted near-miss served from the learned semantic cache is at-least-as-good as the live answer the same request would have produced, at a fixed (near-zero) budget.
slos.rationale: Near-miss reuse is only safe if a served answer is as good as a live one; the acceptance threshold is calibrated from traces so accepted hits clear a precision target. The budget gates 1.0, above the published 0.90 floor.

slos.statement: A near-miss whose similarity is below the calibrated acceptance threshold is never served — the cache falls back to a live call rather than risk a wrong answer.
slos.rationale: The calibrated bar is the safety guarantee; if a below-threshold match could still be served the calibration would be meaningless. The budget gates that no below-bar match is ever served.

slos.statement: The eval-replay regression gate — the cache analogue of the model-swap gate — passes a faithful cache and blocks a drifted one whose near-misses no longer match the live answer.
slos.rationale: A cache that drifts must be caught before it ships, the same way a regressing model swap is; the budget gates that the gate passes a good cache and fails a drifted one.

slos.statement: A LoRA-class adapter fit on-device from the flywheel's promoted dataset is at-least-as-good as its base model on the held-out eval set; it is promoted only behind the same no-regression gate a hosted fine-tune job clears.
slos.rationale: On-device adaptation must never trade quality for locality; the gate is the at-least-as-good guarantee. The budget gates that the adapted model does not regress the base on the eval set.

slos.statement: An on-device adapter reshapes only the traffic it was trained on: an off-distribution request stays inert and falls through to the base model unchanged.
slos.rationale: An adapter that reshaped unseen traffic could silently degrade it; the bound is the safety guarantee. The budget gates that an off-distribution request is answered by the base model, not the adapter.

slos.statement: A regressing on-device adapter is refused — never promoted, never applied, the registry head left on the last known-good version — so adaptation is bounded and reversible.
slos.rationale: Continual adaptation is only safe if a bad version cannot ship; the budget gates that an adapter which regresses the base is refused and the active version is unchanged.

slos.statement: A federated contribution shares what a member learned, never its raw traffic: the wire artifact carries only the numeric subspace scatter — no prompt and no response appears anywhere in it.
slos.rationale: Cross-org improvement is only acceptable if no member's traffic leaves its boundary; the budget gates that a serialized contribution contains none of the member's prompt or response text — companion budgets gate that the contribution's sensitivity is clipped to a bound.

slos.statement: No single member's contribution is recoverable from a federated round: a masked update is indistinguishable from noise on its own, yet the masks cancel exactly in the aggregate so the merged scatter equals the true fleet sum.
slos.rationale: Secure aggregation is the guarantee that the aggregator never sees an individual update; a companion budget gates that a masked individual update differs from the true one (it is hidden), and that a round below the k-anonymity contributor floor is refused.

slos.statement: A merged federated candidate is adopted only when the member's adapter re-fit against the shared subspace is at-least-as-good as its base on the held-out eval set — the same no-regression gate a local promotion and a hosted fine-tune job clear — and is refused and reversible otherwise.
slos.rationale: Sharing across a trust boundary must never trade quality for breadth; the gate is the at-least-as-good guarantee. Companion budgets gate that a regressing federated adapter is refused with the registry head unchanged, and that adoption is reversible.

slos.statement: A member's pull on the consensus geometry is weighted by a reputation earned only from how its past contributions fared against the no-regression gate: discounting a repeatedly-regressing or adversarial member leans the merged subspace toward the reliable members, where an equal-weight merge would let the regressor pull it astray.
slos.rationale: Equal-weight aggregation lets an unreliable member pull the consensus as hard as a reliable one; reputation weighting must measurably discount it. The budget gates that the weighted merge aligns the consensus closer to the reliable member than the unweighted merge — companion budgets gate that the regressor's weight is the smaller, that a weight never leaves [floor, 1] (discounted, never zeroed), and that reputation is reconstructable from the audit chain.

slos.statement: Reputation only ever lowers a member's pull, never bypasses the quality bar: a reliability-weighted federated round still adopts only when at-least-as-good as base, so even a member with a pristine reputation cannot push a regressing adapter through the no-regression gate.
slos.rationale: Weighting changes which geometry the fleet converges toward when every candidate already clears the gate — it must never become a way around the gate. The budget gates that a regressing adapter from pristine-reputation contributors is still refused and reversible; companion budgets gate that the round adopts when at-least-as-good and records its verdict back to the ledger.

slos.statement: A subject's cumulative (ε, δ) privacy loss composes across every memory consolidation and learning round their data touches: the Rényi/moments accountant tracks the running budget across rounds and bounds it more tightly than naively summing each step's ε.
slos.rationale: A per-round bound is not a per-subject guarantee; the accountant must compose across rounds. The budget gates that the cumulative ε grows with each round yet stays below the naive sum — companion budgets gate the exact Gaussian RDP and the monotonicity of the spend.

slos.statement: A memory consolidation or a federated contribution that would push a subject's cumulative (ε, δ) past their privacy budget is refused — the privacy analogue of a hard cost cap — or down-weighted to fit; an over-budget release never silently proceeds.
slos.rationale: A budget that cannot refuse is advisory, not a guarantee. The budget gates that an over-budget step is refused — companion budgets gate that memory consolidation and federated contributions are both gated, that down-weighting keeps the release within the ceiling, and that one subject's spend never touches another's.

slos.statement: The spent privacy budget is a mechanical, auditable number, not a policy doc: a per-subject privacy report sits alongside the cost report, and every spend and refusal is recorded on the hash-chained, verifiable audit log.
slos.rationale: A privacy guarantee must be provable, not asserted. The budget gates that spends and refusals land on the verifiable audit chain — a companion budget gates that the per-subject report exposes spent and remaining ε with operation and refusal counts.

slos.statement: Every run yields a per-run energy (watt-hours) and carbon (grams CO₂e) estimate, accrued deterministically from the run's token accounting against a per-model (by-tier) intensity and a per-region grid factor — the energy analogue of the per-run dollar cost, on the same cost-report surface.
slos.rationale: Sustainability disclosure needs a number, not a gesture: the estimate must be produced for every run and be mechanical. The budget gates that an enabled run reports a positive, reproducible estimate — companion budgets gate that decode dominates prefill, that a stronger tier draws more, that carbon tracks energy at the grid intensity, and that an unknown model still reports non-zero rather than a silent zero.

slos.statement: A run that would push a scope's accrued energy or carbon over its sustainability envelope is refused — the energy analogue of a hard cost cap; an over-budget run never silently proceeds.
slos.rationale: A budget that cannot refuse is advisory, not a guarantee. The budget gates that a carbon envelope refuses the over-budget run — a companion budget gates that an energy (watt-hour) ceiling refuses too, so either unit can cap a run.

slos.statement: The energy/carbon estimate is a mechanical, offline, auditable number: computed in-process from a deterministic intensity table (no external service), on the cost-report surface, with both the per-run estimate and every budget refusal recorded on the hash-chained, verifiable audit log.
slos.rationale: A sustainability figure auditors can trust must be reproducible and on the record, not a black-box service call. The budget gates that the per-run estimate and the refusal both land on the verifiable audit chain and the cost-report surface — companion budgets gate that the report matches the tracker and that accounting is off until explicitly enabled.

slos.statement: The governance invariants — injection containment (untrusted ⇒ no unapproved capability), in-jurisdiction residency, the budget hard cap, and the erasure-proof content binding — are proven to hold across their whole bounded, typed state space by a deterministic in-process verifier, ahead of any run, with every holding invariant checked at every point of its domain (a proof, not a sample).
slos.rationale: A governance guarantee checked by construction beats one observed after the fact. The budget gates that all four invariants hold — companion budgets gate that the check covers the whole domain (states_checked == domain_size), spans all four invariants, and is deterministically reproducible.

slos.statement: A violated governance invariant yields a concrete, delta-minimized counterexample — the input, the labels, the capability gap — rather than a bare verdict, so a governance regression is debuggable; demonstrated by a fail-open residency posture, a projection-blind budget cap, and a bypassed containment gate each exhibiting a minimal witness.
slos.rationale: A verifier that only says 'failed' is not actionable. The budget gates that a violation in each of the residency, budget, and containment invariants produces a counterexample — a companion budget gates that the witness is delta-minimized to its simplest form.

slos.statement: The verification verdict is a mechanical, offline, auditable artifact: a content-hashed report computed in-process with no external prover service, recorded on the hash-chained, verifiable audit log — a holding pass as an allow decision and a violating pass as a deny.
slos.rationale: A proof auditors can trust must be reproducible and on the record. The budget gates that the verdict lands on the verifiable audit chain (allow when held, deny when a misconfigured app is flagged) — companion budgets gate that the report digest re-derives and that a fail-open posture is caught through the app surface.

slos.statement: A video-grounded claim resolves to a time range, not just a document: every clip-evidence citation carries the segment's start/end seconds end-to-end, so an answer is auditable at sub-clip resolution.
slos.rationale: Temporal structure is what makes video evidence; an answer that loses the timestamp is no better than a transcript. The budget gates that every segment grounds to its exact time range, and a companion budget gates that the time range survives into the rendered citation footnote.

slos.statement: Every generated or edited video carries a C2PA manifest bound to its bytes; tampering fails verification — synthetic video is as tamper-evident as a generated image, audio clip, or text answer.
slos.rationale: Synthetic media must be auditable: the manifest binds to the exact bytes a consumer receives. Companion budgets gate that an altered clip fails verification and that an edit is marked synthetic.

slos.statement: Video is a first-class evidence modality: a clip's segments are scored, budgeted, ordered, and cited in the same packet as text and image evidence, with the modality and temporal locator preserved through compilation.
slos.rationale: Video must ride the existing multimodal packet, not a bolt-on plane. The budget gates that clip segments lower to typed video evidence; companion budgets gate that the compiler selects a clip candidate and the packet carries its time range.

slos.statement: The edge / WASM build is the same library under a build target, not a fork: an edge compile is byte-identical to a direct server compile over the same inputs, so a capability can never silently diverge between server and edge.
slos.rationale: Parity is the whole point of an edge build that shares the core: if the edge packet drifted from the server packet, the offline test suite would no longer cover the edge path. The budget gates that the edge runtime and a direct server compile produce the same packet spec hash; companion budgets gate that the runtime delegates to the canonical compiler and rail engine rather than re-implementing them.

slos.statement: The edge runtime holds a bounded resident-memory profile: the compiled packet's footprint stays under the profile cap even as the candidate corpus grows 10×, held by slimming and evidence eviction the way the server's resident-memory budget is.
slos.rationale: A constrained target needs a guaranteed footprint, not a hope. The budget gates that a 4× and a 40× corpus both compile inside the profile's resident and token caps; a companion budget gates that eviction actually fires under the 10× load (some evidence dropped) while the footprint still fits, proving the bound is enforced, not merely unexercised.

slos.statement: The edge core is WASM-buildable: every module on the compile/score/rail/pack path imports no native or optional dependency unconditionally — only the stdlib, pydantic, httpx, and other vincio modules — so the dependency-free core compiles for a browser or edge worker.
slos.rationale: "Runs in your process" extends to a WASM process only if the core pulls nothing native at import time. The budget gates that a static scan of the edge-core modules finds no unconditional import of a native/optional package (NumPy stays behind its guarded pure-Python fallback); a companion budget gates that the scanned module set stays comprehensive.

slos.statement: A consumed MCP server's server-rendered UI resource is surfaced through the existing AG-UI generative-UI channel under the run's governance: each render carries an untrusted-external provenance, is token-metered against the run's budget (an oversized render is refused, not streamed), and is recorded on the hash-chained audit log.
slos.rationale: MCP Apps must ride one streamed run, not open a new ungoverned UI path. The budget gates that a server's ui:// resource becomes an AG-UI mcp.ui event; companion budgets gate that the render carries an untrusted-external trust level, that an oversized render is budget-refused rather than streamed, and that every render lands on the audit chain — so server UI inherits the run's provenance, budget, and audit.

slos.statement: A server's mid-call elicitation request is gated by the same approval and rail machinery that governs a write tool: an approval can deny the request before any value is collected, the collected value is screened through the input rails, and an accepted value is tainted untrusted — so a secret value is refused and an elicited value can never silently authorize a side effect.
slos.rationale: An elicited value is untrusted input from a third-party server; treating it as trusted would reopen the containment hole the dual-plane executor closes. The budget gates that a secret elicited value is refused by the input rail; companion budgets gate that an accepted value is tainted untrusted (contained), that an approval gate denies the request before collection, and that the end-to-end server-tool path is contained — so elicitation inherits the write-tool approval + rail guarantees.

slos.statement: Vincio adopts the evolving MCP spec's surface while staying interoperable: protocol-version negotiation honours a peer pinned to an older stable revision (falling back to the latest Vincio implements for an unknown one), so a capability never silently breaks across a spec-revision boundary.
slos.rationale: The spec moves; interoperability must survive it. The budget gates that negotiation returns a peer's supported revision and falls back for an unsupported one; a companion budget gates that an in-process initialize records the negotiated revision on both peers.

slos.statement: A bounded agent negotiation always terminates within its round/deadline budget: a bargain whose parties' acceptable regions overlap reaches a contract, and one with no overlap ends in a clean no-deal — never an unbounded loop, and a wall-clock deadline returns a partial result.
slos.rationale: Negotiation is the analogue of a bounded crew round; termination must be a guarantee, not a hope. The budget gates that an overlapping bargain agrees within the round budget; companion budgets gate that a no-overlap bargain ends in a bounded no-deal and that a deadline returns a partial result, so a negotiation can never run forever.

slos.statement: A negotiated contract is a typed, signed, offline-verifiable artifact: it is signed by both parties, its content hash recomputes and every signature checks from the bytes alone, a tampered term is caught, and the agreed terms enforce like any other budget.
slos.rationale: A contract is only as good as its tamper-evidence. The budget gates that an agreed contract verifies offline; companion budgets gate that it is signed by both parties, that a tampered term fails verification, and that the terms lower to a budget the runtime enforces — so a contract is a mechanical, verifiable cap, never a self-asserted promise.

slos.statement: A cross-org saga is durable and resumable: its journal is checkpointed after every step, so a fresh process resumes it by id and continues from the cursor — a completed step is never re-run — and the hash-chained journal verifies offline from the bytes alone, catching any tampered record.
slos.rationale: Cross-org work is long-running; it must survive a restart the way an in-process durable graph does. The budget gates that an interrupted saga resumes on a fresh engine sharing the store without re-running a completed step; companion budgets gate that the journal verifies offline and that a tampered record is caught — so a half-finished cross-org transaction is recoverable and tamper-evident, never lost or forged.

slos.statement: A failed cross-org saga unwinds cleanly: a forward step that fails, raises, or breaches its step contract triggers deterministic compensation of the already-completed steps in reverse order, so a half-completed transaction is rolled back rather than left dangling.
slos.rationale: Without compensation a cross-org failure strands a partial transaction across trust boundaries. The budget gates that a failure compensates the completed steps in reverse order; companion budgets gate that a delivered contract breach also compensates and that each side audits its own steps on its own chain — so a saga is an all-or-nothing unit of cross-org work, governed per-org without a shared control plane.

slos.statement: Usage metered against a contract is total-preserving: a meter reading's cost, latency, and usage totals are exactly the sum of the accrued usage events — no double-count, no drop — so a settlement built from a reading is a faithful reconciliation of what was delivered, not an estimate.
slos.rationale: Closing the books is only meaningful if the meter is faithful. The budget gates that a reading's totals equal the sum of its events (cost and latency summed, quality the weakest link held against a floor); companion budgets gate that the resulting settlement reconciles delivery against the agreed terms and that a whole saga's contracted steps settle from the durable journal — so a cross-org engagement reconciles the way a run closes its cost report.

slos.statement: A settlement record is a typed, signed, offline-verifiable reconciliation: both parties sign one reconciliation hash binding the economic facts, the record and the hash-chained settlement book recompute and every signature checks from the bytes alone, a tampered figure is caught, and two parties' independently-produced records tie out (a disagreement is flagged as a dispute).
slos.rationale: A settlement is only as good as its tamper-evidence. The budget gates that a signed settlement verifies offline; companion budgets gate that a tampered figure fails verification, that the hash-chained book recomputes and catches an edited record, that two orgs' records reconcile (and a disagreement is detected), and that the verdict lands on the audit chain — so a cross-org settlement is a mechanical, verifiable ledger of what was owed and delivered, never a self-asserted claim or a hosted clearing house.

slos.statement: A cross-org saga step that declares a capability binds the best-available counterparty at dispatch time: among the allow-listed candidates that advertise the capability and are reachable, the one whose reputation and prior settlement record best fit the step's contract is chosen, deterministically (ties broken by org id), and the decision is recorded on the saga journal.
slos.rationale: Run-time discovery is only useful if it binds the right counterparty. The budget gates that the higher-reputation candidate outranks a regressing one; companion budgets gate that a capability step binds and completes, that the candidate set is surfaced for audit, and that a discovered step is contract-enforced, compensated at the bound org, durable across a restart, and A2A-portable exactly as a statically-wired one — so discovery changes who runs a step, never whether it is bound correctly.

slos.statement: Run-time discovery never relaxes governance: a candidate that advertises the capability but is not allow-listed or has no participant binding is never bound, the governed resolution of every candidate and the binding decision land on the coordinator's hash-chained audit chain, and a capability with no eligible candidate is refused rather than silently dropped.
slos.rationale: Discovery resolves who, but must never bypass how. The budget gates that an unlisted candidate scores zero and is never bound while its resolution is audited; companion budgets gate that an unreachable candidate is rejected, that no eligible candidate raises rather than stalling, and that the binding decision itself is on the chain — so the agent fabric stays as accountable when the counterparty is discovered as when it is wired by id.

slos.statement: Multilateral netting is conservative and minimal: a fleet's net positions sum to zero (every payable is a receivable), the cleared set of obligations reproduces every org's net position exactly, and a web of bilateral obligations clears to fewer transfers than the gross edges — at most one per party less one.
slos.rationale: Clearing is only safe if no value is created or lost. The budget gates that the net positions balance to zero and the cleared obligations reproduce them; companion budgets gate that a cycle clears to fewer transfers than its gross edges, that two opposing flows collapse to one bilateral figure, and that the same settlement seen from both books is deduped rather than double-counted — so an org that is both buyer and seller across many contracts closes its books once, with every position conserved.

slos.statement: A cleared netting set is content-bound and offline-verifiable: a netting hash binds the fleet, the exact source records read, the net positions, and the cleared obligations, the set signs and recomputes from the bytes alone, a tampered figure or a forged source record is caught, and two books that disagree on a contract are pinpointed as a dispute rather than silently netted.
slos.rationale: A clearing is only as good as its tamper-evidence. The budget gates that a signed netting set verifies offline; companion budgets gate that a tampered obligation fails verification, that a tampered source record is refused outright, that two clearers reading the same records compute the same co-signable hash, that a disagreement is pinpointed as a dispute and excluded, and that the clearing lands on the audit chain — so a cleared balance is a mechanical, verifiable artifact, never a self-asserted claim or a hosted clearing house.

slos.statement: Dispute arbitration decides only from what it can recompute: a reconciliation hash that both the buyer and the seller co-signed is upheld with the figure they agreed, a unilateral claim that contradicts it is rejected and its claimant pinpointed, a single uncontested claim stands on its own, a genuine standoff neither side corroborated is left unresolved rather than decided by fiat, and a tampered or forged claim is marked inadmissible rather than crashing the adjudication.
slos.rationale: A resolution is only legitimate if it asserts nothing it cannot prove. The budget gates that a mutually co-signed figure is upheld with the correct balance; companion budgets gate that a contradicting unilateral claim is rejected and its claimant named, that a single uncontested claim stands, that a genuine standoff is left unresolved, and that a tampered or forged claim is marked inadmissible and pinpointed rather than dropped or raised — so a dispute closes the way a settlement does, on verifiable evidence, never on the arbiter's word.

slos.statement: A resolution is content-bound and offline-verifiable: a resolution hash binds the contract, the parties, the outcome, and every adjudicated claim, the resolution signs and recomputes from the bytes alone, a tampered verdict is caught even after re-sealing because the decision re-derives from the recorded claims, two arbiters reading the same records compute the same co-signable hash, and the adjudication lands on the audit chain and closes the reputation loop on the party whose claim did not stand.
slos.rationale: A settled dispute is only as good as its tamper-evidence. The budget gates that a signed resolution verifies offline; companion budgets gate that a tampered verdict fails verification, that a flipped standing flag is caught even after re-sealing because the decision re-derives from the claims, that two arbiters compute the same co-signable hash, and that the adjudication is audited and debits the dissenter — so a resolution is a mechanical, verifiable artifact, never a self-asserted ruling or a hosted court of record.

slos.statement: A portable reputation asserts only what it can recompute and combines without singling anyone out: an attestation summarizes the issuer's own signed settlement and arbitration outcomes (a fulfilled delivery a success, a breach or a dissent a failure), several issuers' evidence pools into one bounded, evidence-weighted prior rather than a single self-asserted number, an issuer that vouches for itself is refused, an issuer cannot stack its own pull, and the imported prior weights a negotiation against an unknown counterparty under the same [floor, 1] rule a local reputation does — discounting a regressor without zeroing or singling it out.
slos.rationale: Portable standing is only trustworthy if no one number can fabricate it. The budget gates that several issuers' attested evidence pools into one prior; companion budgets gate that an attestation summarizes the issuer's own earned outcomes, that more corroborating evidence raises the weight while a regressor stays above the floor, that a self-attestation and a stacked attestation are refused, that an unknown counterparty falls back to the benefit-of-the-doubt prior, and that the imported prior actually weights a negotiation — so reputation travels the fabric as an evidence-weighted prior, never a hosted reputation bureau or a self-asserted score.

slos.statement: An attestation is content-bound and offline-verifiable: an attestation hash binds the issuer, the subject, the evidence counts, the prior, and the source records read, the attestation signs and recomputes from the bytes alone, a tampered score is caught even after re-sealing because the attested reputation re-derives from the evidence, a forged issuer is refused and pinpointed, two importers reading the same attestations compute the same standing, and issuance lands on the audit chain.
slos.rationale: A portable attestation is only as good as its tamper-evidence. The budget gates that a signed attestation verifies offline; companion budgets gate that a tampered score fails verification even after re-sealing because the reputation re-derives from the evidence, that a forged issuer is refused and pinpointed rather than silently dropped, that two importers compute the same standing, and that issuance is audited — so an attested standing is a mechanical, verifiable artifact a prospective counterparty trusts from the bytes alone, never a self-asserted claim or a hosted reputation service.
